{{Short description="Scientific laws describing the motion of planets around the Sun"}}
{{Infobox physical law
| name = Kepler's laws of planetary motion
| image = Kepler laws diagram.svg
| caption = Illustration of Kepler's three laws: elliptical orbits, equal areas, and the period–radius relationship.
| formula = <math>T^2 \propto a^3</math> (third law)
| discovered_by = [[Johannes Kepler]] (1609–1619)
}}

In [[astronomy]], '''Kepler's laws of planetary motion''', published by [[Johannes Kepler]] between 1609 and 1619, describe the orbits of [[planet]]s around the [[Sun]]. The laws modified the [[heliocentric model|heliocentric theory]] of [[Nicolaus Copernicus]], replacing its circular orbits and [[deferent and epicycle|epicycles]] with elliptical trajectories, and explaining how planetary velocities vary.

The three laws state that:
# The orbit of a planet is an [[ellipse]] with the Sun at one of the two [[focus (geometry)|foci]].
# A line segment joining a planet and the Sun sweeps out equal areas during equal intervals of time.
# The square of a planet's [[orbital period]] is proportional to the cube of the length of the [[semi-major and semi-minor axes|semi-major axis]] of its orbit.

== First law (Law of ellipses) ==
:''The orbit of every planet is an ellipse with the Sun at one of the two foci.''

Mathematically, an ellipse with semi-major axis <math>a</math> and eccentricity <math>e</math>:
:<math>r = \frac{a(1 - e^2)}{1 + e\cos\theta}</math>

where <math>r</math> is the distance from the Sun and <math>\theta</math> is the true anomaly.

The [[eccentricity (mathematics)|eccentricity]] <math>e</math> measures deviation from a circle (<math>e = 0</math> for a circle). Most planets have low eccentricities (Earth: <math>e \approx 0.0167</math>).

== Second law (Law of equal areas) ==
:''A line joining a planet and the Sun sweeps out equal areas during equal intervals of time.''

This is a statement of the [[conservation of angular momentum]]:
:<math>\frac{dA}{dt} = \frac{L}{2m} = \text{constant}</math>

Consequence: planets move faster when closer to the Sun ([[perihelion]]) and slower when farther away ([[aphelion]]).

== Third law (Law of harmonies) ==
:''The square of the orbital period of a planet is proportional to the cube of the semi-major axis of its orbit.''

:<math>T^2 \propto a^3</math>

Newton later derived the precise form:
:<math>T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2}{GM}a^3</math>
where <math>G</math> is the [[gravitational constant]] and <math>M</math> is the mass of the central body.

For the Sun: <math>\frac{4\pi^2}{GM_\odot} \approx 2.97 \times 10^{-19}\,\text{s}^2/\text{m}^3</math>.

== Newton's derivation ==
[[Isaac Newton]] showed that Kepler's laws follow mathematically from his [[Newton's law of universal gravitation|law of universal gravitation]] and [[Newton's laws of motion|laws of motion]]. This was a monumental achievement:
* The first law follows from the inverse-square force law (conic sections are the only solutions)
* The second law is a direct consequence of angular momentum conservation
* The third law relates the gravitational constant to orbital parameters

== Applications ==
* Determination of planetary masses using satellite orbits
* Calculation of orbital periods and distances for [[exoplanet]]s
* [[Spacecraft]] trajectory design
* Estimation of the mass of the [[Milky Way]] galaxy from stellar orbits
* [[Binary star]] system analysis

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Newton's law of universal gravitation]]
* [[Orbital mechanics]]
* [[Celestial mechanics]]
* [[Ellipse]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Kepler's laws of planetary motion| ]]
[[Category:Celestial mechanics]]
[[Category:Orbits]]
